planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, thin, oolitic limestone soil in partial shade to full sun; white ironwood grows naturally in the tropical hardwood hammocks of the Florida Keys where it is an uncommon component of the mature hammock canopy. It is rare and declining.

directions: Plant in well-drained, thin, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral oolitic limestone soil in partial shade; white ironwood is a rare native tree of the tropical hardwood hammocks of the Florida Keys, a member of the soapberry family (Sapindaceae) producing trifoliate compound leaves with small, leathery leaflets and small white flower clusters followed by black, fleshy fruits. The black fruit gives the tree its alternate common name 'inkwood.' The extremely hard, dense wood was historically used for construction and tool handles, and the tree is sometimes confused with paradise tree (Simarouba). Critically threatened in the wild due to habitat loss in the Keys, white ironwood is an important species for native plant restoration in the Florida Keys and extreme south Florida. The fruits are eaten by white-crowned pigeons and other subtropical birds. A conservation plant worth growing in appropriate south Florida native gardens.

planting.note: Plant seeds immediately upon collection (they lose viability quickly) or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; big-leaf mahogany requires frost-free conditions and grows naturally in tropical and subtropical forests of Central and South America.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun in frost-free zone 10–12; big-leaf mahogany is the commercial mahogany of global trade — the source of almost all mahogany lumber in furniture, cabinets, boats, and fine woodwork worldwide. Native to tropical forests of Central and South America from Mexico to Brazil, it produces spectacular large, compound leaves up to 12 inches long that unfurl pink-bronze before maturing to glossy dark green. The enormous seed pods hang from the canopy and split to release winged seeds. The species is now protected under CITES Appendix II due to severe overharvesting, with few large trees remaining in accessible forests. In zone 10 Florida, it grows as a large ornamental shade tree, widely planted in Miami-Dade parks and neighborhoods. Planting contributes to conservation of this critically important and over-exploited tropical timber species.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, sandy, or thin soil in full sun to partial shade; Jamaican caper grows naturally in tropical hardwood hammocks, coastal scrub, and coastal strand of south Florida and the Keys, tolerating salt spray and rocky, thin alkaline soils well.

directions: Plant in well-drained, thin, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Jamaican caper (formerly Capparis cynophallophora) is a native Florida and Caribbean shrub or small tree related to the Mediterranean caper (Capparis spinosa), sharing the family's distinctive flowers — large, white, nocturnally fragrant blooms with numerous long, showy purple stamens that create a shaving-brush or firework appearance. The flowers open at night and are gone by mid-morning, attracting moths and long-tongued bees. The fruit, which resembles an elongated sausage, splits to reveal brilliant orange-red flesh containing the seeds — an attractive food source for white-crowned pigeons and other subtropical birds. A salt-tolerant, drought-tolerant native for coastal and hammock-edge gardens in south Florida. The young flower buds could theoretically be pickled like Mediterranean capers but this use is not traditionally practiced.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral coastal soil in full sun; stemodia grows naturally in coastal strand, saltmarsh margins, and disturbed coastal sandy habitats of the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. It tolerates salt, drought, and nutrient-poor sandy soils.

directions: Direct sow in sandy, salty, slightly alkaline to neutral, coastal soil in full sun; stemodia is a small, sprawling annual of coastal sandy habitats from the Gulf Coast to the Caribbean, producing tiny, attractive, two-lipped violet to lavender flowers on small, aromatic, glandular-hairy plants. The plant has some culinary and ethnobotanical interest in the Caribbean as a flavoring herb (used in some Jamaican cooking) and traditional medicine. More importantly it is an ecological component of coastal sand plant communities and a useful groundcover for sandy, salty, nutrient-poor coastal soil where few other flowering plants will grow. The aromatic, glandular foliage has a pleasant scent when brushed. A small, obscure but ecologically interesting native of Gulf Coast beach and dune back-areas, useful for detailed ecological plantings of coastal strand communities.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after removing the fleshy seed coat in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or well-drained soil in full sun; Florida soapberry grows naturally in upland sandy forests and sandhills of the Florida Peninsula and is adapted to poor, sandy, well-drained soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Florida soapberry is a native tree of the Florida Peninsula, growing naturally in upland sandy forests, sandhills, and dry flatwoods. Like its relative western soapberry (S. drummondii), it produces fleshy, translucent, amber fruits high in saponins that lather in water and have been used as natural soap. The pinnately compound leaves turn yellow in fall. Florida soapberry tends to be smaller and more restricted in distribution than western soapberry, naturally adapted to the sandy, acid, well-drained soils of Florida's uplands rather than the calcareous plains soils of the central US. The fruits are eaten by mockingbirds and robins. An underused drought-tolerant native for sandy, dry garden conditions in Florida and adjacent Gulf States where its multiple seasons of interest — spring flowers, fall color, and persistent amber fruits — make it a useful ornamental native.
